Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Bifunctional fructose-6-phosphate,2-kinase/fructose-2,6-bisphosphatase; N-terminal regulatory region; kinase and phosphatase domains
Common_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis; spinach; mangrove; rice; maize; potato
Common_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Spinacia oleracea; Bruguiera gymnorrhiza; Oryza sativa; Zea mays; Solanum tuberosum
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Regulator of photosynthetic carbon partitioning, sucrose synthesis, glycolytic balance, and stress metabolic adaptation
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Typically higher in mature leaves than sink tissues
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Higher in mature leaves; lower in sink tissues
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Drought; osmotic stress; salt stress; shifting light; hypoxia; phosphate starvation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in E. coli; antisense/co-suppression in Arabidopsis and potato; metabolite/activity assays; phosphorylation-site identification; 14-3-3 binding assays
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls Fru-2,6-P2 level and thereby regulates carbon partitioning between sucrose and starch; reduced F2KP lowers Fru-2,6-P2 and favors sucrose formation
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Plant F2KP is the bifunctional enzyme responsible for both synthesis and degradation of Fru-2,6-P2. 2. Reduced F2KP in transgenic Arabidopsis abolished detectable F26BPase activity, supporting that Arabidopsis lacks a separate monofunctional F26BPase. 3. F2KP is allosterically regulated by central metabolites and likely additionally regulated by phosphorylation and 14-3-3 binding. 4. Altering F2KP-dependent Fru-2,6-P2 levels changes leaf carbon partitioning between sucrose and starch.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is a review article synthesizing evidence across species; in plants, only one F2KP gene is generally supported. Arabidopsis hypothetical protein At3g30200 is not considered a functional F2KP gene. F2KP forms tetramers, and N-terminus is required for tetramer assembly and full Fru-6-P affinity.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating F2KP offers a route to tune sucrose/starch partitioning and stress-responsive carbon metabolism, but effects are context dependent and often subtle at whole-plant phenotype level.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altered carbon partitioning; no distinct gross phenotype often reported; species-specific accumulation of sucrose versus hexoses may occur.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: cDNA cloning/sequence data; no global omics accession emphasized in the review

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Bifunctional kinase/phosphatase enzyme with fructose-6-phosphate,2-kinase domain and fructose-2,6-bisphosphatase domain
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis; spinach; mangrove; rice; maize; potato
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Spinacia oleracea; Bruguiera gymnorrhiza; Oryza sativa; Zea mays; Solanum tuberosum
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Solanum tuberosum; Nicotiana tabacum; Kalanchoe daigremontiana
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Regulates cytosolic carbon partitioning by inhibiting cyt-FBPase and activating PFP
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate metabolism / photosynthetic carbon metabolism regulation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for Fru-2,6-P2 level and carbon partitioning
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Fructose-6-phosphate for kinase activity; fructose-2,6-bisphosphate for phosphatase activity
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate from Fru-6-P; fructose-6-phosphate from Fru-2,6-P2
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Phosphorylates fructose-6-phosphate at C-2 to form Fru-2,6-P2 and dephosphorylates Fru-2,6-P2 at C-2 back to Fru-6-P
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms and degrades target product
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Typically higher in mature leaves than sink tissues
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Higher in mature leaves; reduced in sink tissues
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Drought; osmotic stress; salt stress; light shifts; hypoxia; phosphate deficiency
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Purification and activity assays; recombinant expression in E. coli; transgenic antisense/co-suppression; metabolite profiling; phosphorylation analysis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Altering F2KP activity changes Fru-2,6-P2 abundance and redistributes photosynthetic carbon between sucrose and starch
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. F2KP is the definitive bifunctional enzyme making and degrading Fru-2,6-P2 in plants. 2. Arabidopsis plants with reduced F2KP show no detectable F26BPase activity, arguing against a separate monofunctional enzyme. 3. Lower Fru-2,6-P2 caused by reducing F2KP shifts partitioning toward sucrose synthesis. 4. F2KP is strongly controlled by metabolites and likely post-translational modification.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Tetrameric enzyme; N-terminus unique to plants and required for tetramer assembly and high Fru-6-P affinity; conserved His564 required for phosphatase catalysis; Ser220 and Ser303 identified as phosphorylation sites in Arabidopsis.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate to engineer sucrose/starch partitioning and stress-associated carbon allocation in leaves and perhaps sink tissues.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Carbon partitioning changes can lead to hexose accumulation instead of desirable sucrose increases in some species/tissues.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Sequence accessions for cloned cDNAs are reported; no large-scale omics dataset emphasized

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Solanum tuberosum; Ricinus communis; Flaveria linearis
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Solanum tuberosum; Flaveria linearis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major transport and storage carbohydrate; supports growth and carbon allocation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point, inhibited by Fru-2,6-P2
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes fructose-1,6-bisphosphate to fructose-6-phosphate in the sucrose biosynthetic pathway
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for formation of sucrose precursor
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Photosynthetic light period; night starch remobilization context
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic reduction of cyt-FBPase in Arabidopsis, potato, and Flaveria; growth/photosynthesis measurements; Fru-2,6-P2 measurements
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced cyt-FBPase impairs photosynthetic sucrose synthesis and can reduce growth or photosynthesis; low cyt-FBPase is partially counteracted by lower Fru-2,6-P2
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Cyt-FBPase is required for photosynthetic sucrose synthesis. 2. It is strongly inhibited by Fru-2,6-P2. 3. Plants with reduced cyt-FBPase show reduced growth and/or photosynthesis depending on species. 4. Castor bean cyt-FBPase interacts with aldolase, suggesting substrate channeling may affect regulation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because it is a major metabolism gene summarized in the review, although the central focus remains F2KP/Fru-2,6-P2 control rather than cyt-FBPase discovery itself.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for tuning sucrose synthesis, but strong reduction causes growth/photosynthesis penalties.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced growth in Arabidopsis and Flaveria; reduced photosynthesis in potato.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: cDNA sequence information cited in review

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Activates PFP to modulate glycolysis/gluconeogenesis and PPi-linked metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established; physiological role described as enigmatic
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Fructose-6-phosphate + PPi or fructose-1,6-bisphosphate depending on reaction direction
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Fructose-1,6-bisphosphate + Pi or fructose-6-phosphate + PPi depending on direction
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes reversible PPi-dependent interconversion between fructose-6-phosphate and fructose-1,6-bisphosphate
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Acts downstream as a target activated by Fru-2,6-P2 rather than directly forming it
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Different isoforms in P-starved versus P-sufficient Brassica
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Hypoxia; phosphate starvation; darkness; sink tissue metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme assays; transgenic plants with reduced PFP activity; metabolite analysis
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: PFP alpha subunit; PFP beta subunit
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activated by Fru-2,6-P2 in both directions, contributes to glycolytic metabolism, but reducing PFP often causes only small carbon flux changes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Plant PFP is activated by Fru-2,6-P2 and Fru-1,6-P2. 2. PFP operates near equilibrium and may function in glycolysis, gluconeogenesis, or PPi balance. 3. Transgenic reduction confirms involvement in glycolytic metabolism but physiological impact is often modest. 4. Alpha and beta subunits likely have regulatory and catalytic specialization, respectively.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Core gene in the review as a major Fru-2,6-P2 target, but not the primary enzyme making/degrading the metabolite.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential component for engineering PPi-saving glycolysis under stress, though benefits remain species- and condition-dependent.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Small or ambiguous metabolic flux responses after manipulation; role context dependent.
